source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Two peaks can just barely be resolved if their spacing $\delta y$ equals the width w of each peak, where w is measured at half of the peak’s height. Two peaks closer together than w will merge into a single peak. We can use this idea to understand the resolution of a diffraction grating. Ordinary hydrogen atoms emit red light with a wavelength of 656.45 nm. In deuterium, which is a “heavy” isotope of hydrogen, the wavelength is 656.27 nm. What is the minimum number of slits in a diffraction grating that can barely resolve these two wavelengths in the first-order diffraction pattern?
